text: As seen here, the prosthetic valve leaflets are thickened and infected with vegetations.
The root abscess is immediately noted at the left-right commissure before the valve is even removed.
During removal of the valve, many annular sutures and pledges were easily removed intact with the valve using just gentle pulling and blunt dissection.
During this, a second large pocket of PureLens was encountered at the right non-coronary commissure.
With the prosthesis removed, one can clearly see the intact annular sutures remaining on the sole and cuff of the valve.
Next, the aortic root was exposed by cutting down on the non-coronary sinus in order to debride the remaining infected tissues.

The root abscess and surrounding phlegmon extended from the right non-coronary commissure towards the left non-coronary commissure, then directly below the left coronary ostium and ending at the left right coronary commissure.
Here we see the aortic root after extensive debridement.
Note the extremely fragile nature of the tissues surrounding the left coronary button.
Prior to patch placement, the Cooley fistula was prepared via a large right atriotomy which was snared down using a pressuring suture.
Now, a 10 by 16 centimeter patch of bovine pericardium was opened and partially tailored.

Using a double-loaded 4-0SH proline suture, the patch was sutured to the heart, starting at the right ventricle and right ventricular outflow tract, over the pulmonary artery and the nearby pericardium, onto the aortic arch and the innominate vein, then down onto the superior vena cava and the surrounding pericardium.

The patch was then further tailored and the second arm of the 4-0 proline suture was used to continue the Cabral patch anastomosis onto the anterior wall of the right ventricle and finally over the right atrium.
Just prior to the completion of the Cabral patch, the snare was removed from the right atrium, the atriotomy was confirmed to be easily opened, and the Cabral patch was completed.
